questions.question_text: After a $25\%$ increase, a price is $\$60$. What percentage of the original does $\$60$ represent?

options.why: Subtracted the increase from $100\%$, as if it were a decrease.
Gave the change percentage instead of what the new value represents.
Treated the new value as still equal to the original.

questions.question_text: After a $30\%$ decrease, a value is $140$. What percentage of the original does $140$ represent?

options.why: Added the change to $100\%$, as if it were an increase.
Gave the size of the decrease instead of what the new value represents.
Treated the new value as still equal to the original.

questions.question_text: After a $150\%$ increase, a value is $500$. What percentage of the original does $500$ represent?

options.why: Gave the size of the increase instead of what the new value represents.
Subtracted $100\%$ from the change instead of adding the change to $100\%$.
Treated the new value as still equal to the original.

questions.question_text: A quantity increased by $40\%$. The increase was $60$. What percentage of the original does the $60$ represent?

options.why: Read $60$ as the new value rather than as the amount of the increase.
Subtracted $40$ from $100$ instead of recognising $60$ as the change itself.
Treated the increase amount as the whole original quantity.

questions.solution_text:
'Increased by $60$' names the change, not the new value.
$60$ represents $40\%$ of the original.

questions.question_text: After a $12\%$ reduction, the number of items left is $60$. What percentage of the original does $60$ represent?

options.why: Added the change to $100\%$, as if it were an increase.
Gave the size of the reduction instead of what the remaining value represents.
Subtracted the wrong amount, taking $8\%$ off instead of $12\%$.
